programs.name: HUSKY C
programs.tagline: Connecticut Medicaid for adults 65+ or disabled, covering medical care plus long-term services like home care.

localEntities.name: Department of Social Services (DSS) offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ or disabled and has limited income and assets, HUSKY C provides comprehensive Medicaid coverage in Connecticut. This includes all standard medical care plus long-term services and supports (LTSS) like home care, adult day programs, and nursing home care when needed.

Unlike other HUSKY programs, HUSKY C has strict asset limits — your parent can only have $1,600 in countable assets as a single person ($2,400 for couples). Income limits vary by program tier: general coverage requires income under $835/month for singles, while the long-term services tier allows up to $2,982/month. Even if your parent's income or assets are slightly over the limits, they may still qualify through a "spend-down" process using medical expenses.

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Access Health CT or call 1-855-805-4325 — processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect birth certificate or driver's license, Social Security card, pay stubs, bank statements, and other income documentation (VA or SSA letters).

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at accesshealthct.com or call 1-855-805-4325. You can also apply directly through your local Department of Social Services office.

steps.title: Complete assessment
steps.description: DSS will review your parent's income, assets, and medical needs. For long-term services, expect a more detailed clinical assessment.

steps.title: Receive decision
steps.description: If approved, coverage typically begins the month of application. If denied due to excess income/assets, ask about spend-down options.

applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if your parent's income or assets are slightly over the limits — the spend-down option lets them qualify by deducting medical expenses.

tiers.name: General Coverage (Aged/Blind/Disabled)
tiers.description: Basic Medicaid health services for adults 65+ or disabled
tiers.coverage: Standard medical care, prescriptions, hospital services

tiers.name: Long-Term Services & Supports (LTSS)
tiers.description: Enhanced coverage including home care and nursing facility services
tiers.coverage: All medical care plus home/community services, nursing facilities, residential care

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than your primary home
Promissory notes and loans owed to your parent
excluded: Primary residence (home your parent lives in)

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs for any earned income
Social Security or VA benefit letters
Documentation of any other income sources

contentSections.text: The $1,600 asset limit often disqualifies people who would otherwise benefit. However, Connecticut has proposed increasing this to $5,000 for singles and $7,500 for couples starting in 2026.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for HUSKY C?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary residence doesn't count toward the $1,600 asset limit, so they can keep their home and still qualify for HUSKY C.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through 'spend-down.' Connecticut allows people to deduct medical expenses from their income to meet the eligibility threshold. Apply anyway — DSS will calculate if this option works.

faqs.question: How is HUSKY C different from Medicare?
faqs.answer: HUSKY C covers services Medicare doesn't, especially long-term care like home health aides, adult day programs, and extended nursing home stays. Your parent can have both Medicare and HUSKY C.

faqs.question: What happens to my parent's retirement accounts?
faqs.answer: IRAs and 401(k)s count toward the $1,600 asset limit. Your parent may need to spend down these accounts first, though there are rules about how quickly this must happen.

faqs.question: Does HUSKY C have a waiting list?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike some Medicaid waiver programs, HUSKY C is an entitlement program. If your parent qualifies, they receive coverage immediately upon approval.

faqs.question: Can immigrants qualify for HUSKY C?
faqs.answer: Qualified non-citizens can apply, but most must wait 5 years after receiving their status. Refugees and asylees don't have this waiting period and can apply immediately.

programs.name: Connecticut Home Care Program for Elders (CHCPE)
programs.tagline: Get 10-70 hours per week of home care services so your parent can stay home instead of moving to a nursing facility.
programs.intro: If your parent is 65+ and needs help with daily activities like bathing or dressing, Connecticut's Home Care Program can provide 10-70 hours per week of home care services. Because this program is designed to prevent nursing home placement, your parent must demonstrate they need hands-on assistance with at least one Activity of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or medication management). The program has three tiers with different income limits and service levels: respite care for family caregivers, state-funded care with no income limit but sliding fees, and Medicaid-funded care with strict income limits ($1,956/month for singles) but more comprehensive coverage.

summary: Needs help with bathing, dressing, or other daily activities
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need assistance with at least 1 Activity of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring from bed/chair, or medication management) for state-funded tier, or 3+ ADLs for nursing facility level of care
otherRequirements: At risk of nursing home placement

applicationGuide.summary: Complete the online Request Form, then a health screening within 48 hours.

steps.title: Submit Request Form
steps.description: Complete the online Request Form at the Connecticut portal with basic information about your parent's care needs

steps.title: Health Screening
steps.description: A brief health screen will be completed within 48 hours of your request submission

steps.title: Provide Documentation
steps.description: Submit income documentation, proof of Connecticut residency, and evidence of functional abilities/ADL needs

steps.title: Assessment
steps.description: Local Area Agency on Aging will complete the full application review and determine program tier eligibility

applicationGuide.processingTime: Health screen completed within 48 hours; full processing time varies by region
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent's income is too high for Medicaid-funded services, they may still qualify for state-funded services with sliding fee scale.

tiers.name: Category 1: Respite Care
tiers.description: Temporary care to give family caregivers a break

tiers.name: Category 2: State-Funded
tiers.description: Adult day care and home care services

tiers.name: Category 3: Medicaid-Funded
tiers.coverage: Up to 70 hours/week or live-in care possible

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary residence (home where your parent lives)

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Connecticut driver's license or state ID
Proof of Connecticut residency (utility bill, lease)

categories.name: Medical/Functional
items: Documentation of care needs from doctor or social worker
List of Activities of Daily Living your parent needs help with

contentSections.text: For Medicaid-funded services (Category 3), Connecticut applies a 5-year financial lookback. Any large gifts or asset transfers in the past 5 years could affect eligibility.
If your parent is married, only their income counts toward the limit — not their spouse's income. This can help with qualification even if household income seems too high.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the $1,956 Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify for state-funded services (Category 2) which has no income limit. They'll pay on a sliding fee scale based on income, but can still receive adult day care and 10-20 hours of home care per week.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary residence is exempt from asset limits. However, for Medicaid-funded services, there may be estate recovery after death to recoup costs from the home's value.

faqs.question: How many hours of care can my parent actually get?
faqs.answer: It depends on the program tier. State-funded typically covers 10-20 hours per week. Medicaid-funded can provide up to 70 hours per week or even live-in care for those with the highest needs.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to be almost ready for a nursing home to qualify?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. For state-funded services, they need help with just 1 Activity of Daily Living. For Medicaid-funded services with higher coverage, they need help with 3+ activities or meet 'nursing facility level of care.'

faqs.question: What's the cost-sharing requirement?
faqs.answer: Participants may be required to pay a 9% cost share or applied income. The exact amount depends on income level and program category.

faqs.question: My parent lives in assisted living. Can they still get CHCPE services?
faqs.answer: The program is designed for people living at home who are at risk of nursing home placement. Contact your local Area Agency on Aging to discuss whether assisted living residents can access certain services.

programs.name: Connecticut PACE Programs
programs.tagline: All-inclusive medical care, adult day services, transportation, and home care — no copays once enrolled if your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home.

localEntities.name: Connecticut-specific PACE providers (search Medicare.gov or CT Dept. of Social Services for current list; not enumerated in results)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and clinically needs nursing home level care but wants to stay at home, PACE provides everything under one program: primary care, hospital care, prescription drugs, physical therapy, adult day care, home care, transportation, meals, and care coordination. Because PACE covers all services through one team, your parent won't need to juggle multiple providers or worry about copays for covered services.

PACE has no income or asset limits for enrollment, but it's only available in specific Connecticut service areas. Most participants are 76+ with multiple chronic conditions. The state must certify that your parent needs nursing home level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar needs), even though they'll receive care at home or in adult day settings.

summary: Needs nursing home level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Connecticut as needing nursing home level of care (daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Live in a Connecticut PACE service area
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prepayment plan, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Connecticut PACE organization directly — processing time not specified but waitlists may apply.

steps.title: Find your local PACE provider
steps.description: Use Medicare.gov PACE finder or contact CT Department of Social Services to locate PACE organizations serving your parent's address.

steps.title: Contact the PACE center
steps.description: Call directly to schedule an assessment and confirm they're accepting new participants.

steps.title: Complete nursing home level of care assessment
steps.description: Connecticut will evaluate whether your parent meets the clinical criteria for nursing home care.

steps.title: Provide enrollment documents
steps.description: Bring proof of age, local residency, Medicare/Medicaid cards, and current health plan information.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible waitlists depending on local program capacity
applicationGuide.tip: Call Medicare.gov at 1-800-MEDICARE (1-800-633-4227) if you can't locate Connecticut PACE providers online.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (Medicaid Coverage Only)
included: Additional real estate beyond primary home
excluded: Life insurance with face value under $1,500

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Connecticut address in PACE service area

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in specific Connecticut service areas. If your parent lives outside a PACE coverage zone, they won't qualify regardless of their care needs.
Don't confuse this with ConnPACE (Connecticut's prescription drug program for 65+). Connecticut PACE is comprehensive medical care for people who need nursing home level care but want to stay home.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house and still qualify for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE itself has no asset limits. Your mom's home doesn't count toward any financial requirements. If she wants Medicaid to help pay for PACE, that has separate asset rules, but the home is still exempt.

faqs.question: What if Dad has Medicare Advantage — can he switch to PACE?
faqs.answer: He'll need to disenroll from Medicare Advantage first. You cannot be in Medicare Advantage and PACE at the same time. Contact his Medicare Advantage plan to cancel before applying to PACE.

faqs.question: How much does PACE cost if Mom doesn't qualify for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: PACE offers private pay through monthly premiums, but Connecticut-specific costs aren't published. Contact your local PACE provider for pricing. Many families find the comprehensive coverage worth the cost compared to paying for multiple services separately.

faqs.question: What if the closest PACE center is 30 minutes away?
faqs.answer: PACE includes transportation to and from the adult day center and medical appointments. Distance may affect eligibility since you must live in the service area, but transportation is provided once enrolled.

faqs.question: Can Dad use his regular doctor with PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE requires using their medical team and providers. Your dad would need to switch to PACE doctors, but the program covers all his medical care coordination in one place.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom gets better and no longer needs nursing home level care?
faqs.answer: She would no longer qualify for PACE. The program requires ongoing certification of nursing home level care needs. PACE will help transition her care if her condition improves significantly.
